perturbation.original_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, and for such purpose Netzee will use commercially reasonable efforts to make and include (without limitation) as such modifications changes and upgrades necessary to comply with generally applicable industry and regulatory requirements of which it is notified
perturbation.changed_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) maintenance upgrades and modifications offered to its customers generally, and for such purpose Netzee will endeavor to consider including as such modifications changes and upgrades that it believes are helpful in complying with industry and regulatory requirements of which it is generally aware
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'standard maintenance upgrades' to 'maintenance upgrades' removes the assurance of a standard level of service. Replacing 'will use commercially reasonable efforts' with 'will endeavor to consider including' makes the compliance effort discretionary. Removing 'without limitation' gives them an out. Changing 'necessary to comply' to 'that it believes are helpful in complying' introduces subjectivity and reduces the obligation. Replacing 'of which it is notified' to 'of which it is generally aware' diminishes the burden to actively maintain knowledge. These weaken the obligation to comply with regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the GLBA, which requires financial institutions to implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program to protect customer information. The ambiguous language weakens Netzee's obligation to comply with regulatory requirements, potentially leaving Bankers Bank vulnerable to non-compliance with GLBA's security mandates. The original text suggested a clear intent to comply with regulatory changes of which it was notified, but the altered version allows Netzee to subjectively decide what is helpful, diminishing their legal obligation.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 94 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 94 : PRIVACY From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 94 —PRIVACY SUBCHAPTER I—DISCLOSURE OF NONPUBLIC PERSONAL INFORMATION Sec. 6801. Protection of nonpublic personal information. 6802. Obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information. 6803. Disclosure of institution privacy policy. 6804. Rulemaking. 6805. Enforcement. 6806. Relation to other provisions. 6807. Relation to State laws. 6808. Study of information sharing among financial affiliates. 6809. Definitions. SUBCHAPTER II—FRAUDULENT ACCESS TO FINANCIAL INFORMATION 6821. Privacy protection for customer information of financial institutions. 6822. Administrative enforcement. 6823. Criminal penalty. 6824. Relation to State laws. 6825. Agency guidance. 6826. Reports. 6827. Definitions. SUBCHAPTER I—DISCLOSURE OF NONPUBLIC PERSONAL INFORMATION §6801. Protection of nonpublic personal information (a) Privacy obligation policy It is the policy of the Congress that each financial institution has an affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information. (b) Financial institutions safeguards In furtherance of the policy in subsection (a), each agency or authority described in section 6805(a) of this title , other than the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection, shall establish appropriate standards for the financial institutions subject to their jurisdiction relating to administrative, technical, and physical safeguards— (1) to insure the security and confidentiality of customer records and information; (2) to protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such records; and (3) to protect against unauthorized access to or use of such records or information which could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer. ( Pub. L. 106–102, title V, §501, Nov. 12, 1999, 113 Stat. 1436 ; Pub. L. 111–203, title X, §1093(1), July 21, 2010, 124 Stat. 2095 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2010 —Subsec. (b). Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to comply with regulatory requirements, creating a contradiction. The original text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts to make and include (without limitation) as such modifications changes and upgrades necessary to comply,' indicating a strong commitment. The changed text uses 'endeavor to consider including as such modifications changes and upgrades that it believes are helpful in complying,' which is subjective and discretionary. This shift contradicts the GLBA's requirement for financial institutions to 'protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (15 USC Ch. 94 §6801). The revised language introduces ambiguity and weakens the responsibility, potentially leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: (3) in the event of material discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services (which shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form), reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans) to correct those discrepancies
perturbation.changed_text: (3) in the event of discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services, effort, as Netzee sees fit, to address discrepancies
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'material', any discrepancy is now a potential issue. Replacing 'reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans)' with 'effort, as Netzee sees fit' allows Netzee complete discretion in addressing discrepancies. Omitting the condition that 'specifications for such Services shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form', the specification is now completely open to interpretation, further giving Netzee discretion to dictate the acceptable condition of discrepancies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability, which requires goods (including software and services) to be fit for the ordinary purposes for which they are used. The ambiguous language regarding discrepancy correction allows Netzee to provide a service that does not meet basic performance standards, undermining the warranty. The original text contained a more definite obligation to correct material discrepancies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to correct discrepancies in services, creating a potential contradiction with the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability. The original text requires 'reasonable effort' to correct 'material discrepancies,' while the changed text allows 'effort, as Netzee sees fit, to address discrepancies,' removing any objective standard. UCC § 2-314(2)(c) states that goods must be 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used'. By allowing Netzee complete discretion in addressing discrepancies, the modified text allows for a service that does not meet basic performance standards, undermining the implied warranty of merchantability.

perturbation.original_text: and (4) availability of basic Services with at least 99% uptime between 4:00 a.m Monday and 7:00 p.m. Friday and between 8:00 a.m. Saturday and 2:00 p.m. Saturday Eastern Time, exclusive of outages caused by off-peak prescheduled maintenance or causes beyond Netzee's reasonable control (including third-party equipment or communications).
perturbation.changed_text: and (4) availability of Services with suitable uptime between hours that Netzee defines, exclusive of outages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'basic' diminishes the quality expectation and allows for the inclusion of lower quality services. Removing the uptime target of 'at least 99%' makes the target vague. Replacing the defined time windows with 'hours that Netzee defines' grants Netzee full control of when service is available. Removing the definition of what constitutes a cause beyond Netzee's reasonable control makes this clause ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the Georgia Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act (O.C.G.A. § 10-1-372) as it creates a likelihood of confusion or misunderstanding regarding the availability and quality of services. The original text promised a specific uptime and service window, whereas the modified text allows Netzee to arbitrarily define these parameters, potentially misleading Bankers Bank about the actual service they are receiving. This change could be interpreted as a deceptive trade practice under Georgia law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine the original contract's clarity and enforceability, creating a contradiction under the definition provided. The original text specified 'basic Services' with 'at least 99% uptime' within defined time windows. The changed text replaces this with 'Services with suitable uptime between hours that Netzee defines', granting Netzee broad discretion. The Perturbation Explanation notes this diminishes quality expectations, makes the uptime target vague, and gives Netzee full control of service availability. This shift to vague and discretionary terms makes it difficult to enforce specific performance standards. The Law Explanation argues this creates a 'likelihood of confusion or misunderstanding' violating Georgia law, as the original terms promised specific service levels while the modified terms allow arbitrary changes. While the scraped law snippets are not useful, the LLM's explanation alongside the perturbation explanation make the contradiction explicit.
